Idiom (Jinghpaw): Aga lai yang hka tai, Ajum lai yang sha n mai.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Balanced conditional proverb; two parallel clauses with lai yang expressing excess or overdoing, followed by consequence clauses.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Careless or excessive speech causes trouble, just as over-fermentation spoils food; moderation and verbal restraint are wise.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ga tsun shaga ai shaloi sadi let tsun ra ai.
Usage Contexts: Family advice; conflict mediation; public speech; humor; community meetings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional Jinghpaw speech wisdom using a food/fermentation image familiar in village and household life.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /a.ɡa lai jaŋ kʰa tai | a.dʑum lai jaŋ ʃa n̩ mai/
First Example Sentence: Makau grup hta ninghkring n lu yang, aga lai yang hka tai, ajum lai yang sha n mai ngu tsun chye ra ai.
Second Example Sentence: Num ningnan ni ga hkum lai hkra, sara gaw aga lai yang hka tai, ajum lai yang sha n mai ngu sadi jaw ai.
Third Example Sentence: Buga up hkang hta ga law law tsun ai majaw manghkang byin wa ai shaloi, duwa wa dai ga malai hpe bai shadum ai.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Dayauk da chyik, Lanyau myaw chik.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical paired clause with repeated compact verb-final rhythm; proverb compresses a cooperative action into two short clauses.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Even small contributions matter; when many people help a little, a large task can be completed.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Masha ni jawm galaw yang bungli kaba mung ngut wa lu ai.
Usage Contexts: Community work; team encouragement; school activities; workplace cooperation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional communal-labor proverb drawing on small creatures and small acts as images of collective strength.
First Example Sentence: Shat shadu ai bungli hta marai langai mi sha n galaw lu, dayauk da chyik, lanyau myaw chik re.
Second Example Sentence: Jinghpaw sharin hpung hta anhte yawng yawm yawm garum yang, dayauk da chyik, lanyau myaw chik.
Third Example Sentence: Kayin gaw kachyi sha rai tim, dayauk da chyik, lanyau myaw chik ngu ai hte maren, shi a garum ai gaw ahkyak ai.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Hka garai n kaba yang, mahkrai htan ra ai.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Temporal conditional clause followed by obligation phrase; n kaba yang marks action before a situation becomes serious.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Solve a problem early while it is still manageable; prevention is easier than emergency repair.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Manghkang kaba n byin shi yang shawng galaw shangut ra ai.
Usage Contexts: Planning; health advice; project management; farming; household maintenance
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: River and bridge imagery reflects highland travel, rainy-season risk, and the value of timely preparation.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /kʰa ɡa.rai n̩ ka.ba jaŋ | ma.kʰrai tʰan ra ai/
First Example Sentence: Project hpe hpang de n da u; hka garai n kaba yang, mahkrai htan ra ai.
Second Example Sentence: Ana n kaba shi yang hkamja lam hpe makawp maga u, hka garai n kaba yang, mahkrai htan ra ai.
Third Example Sentence: Nta gawng n hkyem shi yang shatsawm u ngu, wa gaw hka garai n kaba yang, mahkrai htan ra ai ngu tsun ai.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Hpunau myit hkrum hpyen n shang, Wuloi wula myit hkrum sharaw n dang.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Parallel compound proverb; repeated myit hkrum frames coordinated subjects and negated result clauses.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Unity protects people from danger; a family, village, or group that agrees internally is hard to defeat.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Masha ni myit hkrum mangrum nga yang makawp maga lu ai.
Usage Contexts: Politics; family unity; village leadership; conflict resolution; organizational culture
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kinship and animal imagery encode a social ethic of solidarity across Jinghpaw-speaking communities.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /pʰu.nau mjit kʰrum pʰjen n̩ ʃaŋ | wu.loi wu.la mjit kʰrum ʃa.raw n̩ daŋ/
First Example Sentence: Manau poi hpe galaw na matu hpunau myit hkrum hpyen n shang, wuloi wula myit hkrum sharaw n dang ngu myit hkrum ga.
Second Example Sentence: Company kata myit n hkrum yang bungli hkrat wa na; hpunau myit hkrum hpyen n shang ngu ai hpe matsing da ga.
Third Example Sentence: Buga masha ni myit hkrum wa ai majaw, hpunau myit hkrum hpyen n shang, wuloi wula myit hkrum sharaw n dang ngu ai gaw teng ai.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Hpyen wa a kumhpa gaw hkrit hpa re.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive noun phrase plus topic marker gaw and equative predicate re; compact cautionary statement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A gift or favor from an enemy may be dangerous; hidden motives must be examined before accepting benefits.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Hpyen wa kaw na lu ai hpe sadi maja let hkap la ra ai.
Usage Contexts: Politics; negotiations; business; family disputes; strategic advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Conflict-era wisdom about alliances, gifts, and obligation; often applicable to dealings with rivals.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /pʰjen wa a kum.pʰa ɡaw kʰrit pʰa re/
First Example Sentence: Hpyen wa a kumhpa gaw hkrit hpa re majaw, dai hkap la na lam hpe n kam hkum la ai.
Second Example Sentence: Bungli hpe jahten ai masha kaw na garum lu yang mung, hpyen wa a kumhpa gaw hkrit hpa re ngu dum ra ai.
Third Example Sentence: Dai ndai kumhpa n re, hpyen wa a kumhpa gaw hkrit hpa re ngu nna, shi gaw laika hpe shawng hti ai.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Lagawn e lu ai gaw yup; Yup e lu ai gaw ngak; Ngak e lu ai gaw hkaw; Hkaw e lu ai gaw htung; Htung e lu ai gaw du u.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Cumulative chain proverb; each clause turns the previous result into the next topic, forming a moral cause-and-effect sequence.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Small laziness can grow into serious decline; neglected habits may lead to weakness, illness, or ruin.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Lagawn hkum byin; shakut let hkamja ai lam hpe makawp maga ra ai.
Usage Contexts: Child guidance; moral teaching; health; work discipline; religious or literary speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A long-form household teaching proverb that uses repetition to warn children and youth against idleness.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /la.ɡawn e lu ai ɡaw jup | jup e lu ai ɡaw ŋak | ŋak e lu ai ɡaw kʰaw | kʰaw e lu ai ɡaw tʰuŋ | tʰuŋ e lu ai ɡaw du u/
First Example Sentence: Ma ni hpe sharin shaga shaloi, lagawn e lu ai gaw yup; yup e lu ai gaw ngak ngu ai hpe n-gun jaw ai.
Second Example Sentence: Nhtoi shagu bungli n galaw ai gaw lagawn e lu ai gaw yup ngu ai zawn hpang de jamjau du wa ai.
Third Example Sentence: Shinggyim prat hta lagawn hpe n lu shalawng, lagawn e lu ai gaw yup; yup e lu ai gaw ngak; ngak e lu ai gaw hkaw ngu sadi jaw ai.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Marai masum myit hkrum yang, Panglai nawng pyi htawk dang ai.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional unity clause followed by hyperbolic result; marai masum sets a small group against an immense object.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A few people with a shared mind can accomplish what seems impossible.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Myit hkrum yang bungli yak ai mung awng dang lu ai.
Usage Contexts: Teamwork; leadership; education; activism; family planning
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The image of draining a vast lake or sea emphasizes collective resolve in oral wisdom and community labor.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /ma.rai ma.sum mjit kʰrum jaŋ | paŋ.lai nawŋ pji tʰawk daŋ ai/
First Example Sentence: Team masum sha nga tim, marai masum myit hkrum yang, panglai nawng pyi htawk dang ai.
Second Example Sentence: Sharin hpung hta myit hkrum wa yang, marai masum myit hkrum yang, panglai nawng pyi htawk dang ai ngu hkap yin ai.
Third Example Sentence: Myit n hkrum yang aloi bungli mung yak wa ai; myit hkrum yang panglai nawng pyi htawk dang ai.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Masha garum ai hpe matsing da u, Tinang garum ai hpe malap da u.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Double imperative proverb; object-marked clauses contrast remembering others' help with forgetting one's own help.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Be grateful for help received, but do not boast about help you gave.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Chyeju hpe matsing da nna tinang a garum ai hpe n shadawng ra ai.
Usage Contexts: Ethics; family instruction; workplace humility; friendship; community service
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A gratitude-and-humility saying associated with reciprocity norms in close-knit Kachin/Jinghpaw social life.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /ma.ʃa ɡa.rum ai pʰe mat.siŋ da u | ti.naŋ ɡa.rum ai pʰe ma.lap da u/
First Example Sentence: Chyeju dum ai myit nga na matu, masha garum ai hpe matsing da u, tinang garum ai hpe malap da u.
Second Example Sentence: Shi gaw shingran kaja rai nna, masha garum ai hpe matsing da u, tinang garum ai hpe malap da u ngu ai hpe prat hta jai lang ai.
Third Example Sentence: Nang a sharin laika hta garum ya ai masha ni hpe malap hkum; masha garum ai hpe matsing da u.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Matsan nngai ngu hkum hkrap, Lu nngai ngu hkum kadap.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative imperative pair; hkum prohibits emotional extremes, balancing complaint and boasting.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not merely lament poverty or boast of wealth; keep humility and work steadily.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A matsan hpe hkrap hkum, a lu su hpe arawng hkum mala; shakut ra ai.
Usage Contexts: Moral advice; economic hardship; youth guidance; marketplace talk; sermons
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A social-balance proverb warning against both despair and arrogance in changing economic conditions.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /mat.san n̩.ŋai ŋu kʰum kʰrap | lu n̩.ŋai ŋu kʰum ka.dap/
First Example Sentence: Matsan nngai ngu hkum hkrap, lu nngai ngu hkum kadap ngu ai gaw kaji kaja de shakut na matu n-gun jaw ai.
Second Example Sentence: Bazaar hta ja law law lu ai shaloi mung, lu nngai ngu hkum kadap ngu nna, shi n jawng ai.
Third Example Sentence: Nta hta jamjau nga tim, matsan nngai ngu hkum hkrap ngu ai hte maren, anhte hkum hkrit hkum ra ai.

Idiom (Jinghpaw): Myit yang mang ai, galaw yang dang ai
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Antithetical conditional pair; myit yang contrasts with galaw yang, ending in opposing result predicates.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Thinking without action creates confusion, while doing the work reveals the solution and leads to success.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Myit sha n nga ai; galaw shakut yang mahtai lu ai.
Usage Contexts: Student motivation; work coaching; project starts; everyday encouragement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Action-oriented oral wisdom emphasizing practical effort over hesitation; useful in learning, farming, and work.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Jinghpaw): /mjit jaŋ maŋ ai | ɡa.law jaŋ daŋ ai/
First Example Sentence: Research hpe myit sha myit nga yang mang ai; galaw yang dang ai.
Second Example Sentence: Nang project hpe hpang na matu n hkrit u, myit yang mang ai, galaw yang dang ai.
Third Example Sentence: Shinggyim hkumga hta mung, myit yang mang ai, galaw yang dang ai ngu ai gaw ahkyak ai.
